From a NOAA page:
Glenda Kuehn I actually just recently put up a post on my page explaining this in detail so I'll copy/paste it below for anyone here who is interested...
While it is true that the majority of hurricanes move more toward the west at this latitude, easterly motions are not unheard of. First, it's important to note WHY most hurricanes spend the majority of their time moving east to west. Most hurricanes, unsurprisingly, spend the majority of their time in the tropics between 0N and 30N latitude. That region of the planet is dominated by a general easterly flow pattern thanks to the easterly trade winds. These winds are a product of the coriolis effect and the low-level section of the Hadley cell, which is the southern most of the 3 major circulation cells that drive large scale motions in Earth's atmosphere. However, if you live in the mid-latitudes where most of the US is located, you'll notice the majority of your weather travels from west to east, in the opposite direction. Unlike the tropics, the mid-latitudes are dominated by the mid-latitude westerlies which are an extension of the Ferrel cell; the second of the 3 major cells.
If you pay attention to most hurricane tracks, they will often move east to west in the tropics thanks to the easterly trade winds and then "recurve" to the north and then east as they gain latitude and move into the mid-latitude westerlies. However, as we get into October and November during the fall, the Hadley cell contracts and the mid-latitude westerlies will start to make it farther south. This means easterly or "backward" motion is more likely
with hurricanes farther to the south.
In Milton's case, a low amplitude shortwave trough embedded in the westerlies will make it into the northern Gulf of Mexico, far enough south to impart an easterly motion on the Hurricane and drag it toward Florida from the west. The front associated with this feature has already been analyzed by the WPC in the northern Gulf. This easterly motion is not unheard of, especially later in the season. There are quite a few examples of tropical cyclones moving more easterly at low latitudes and unsurprisingly, they are almost all in October or November. Hurricane Opal 1997, and Wilma 2005, had a large easterly component of motion in the Gulf of Mexico. Hurricane Michelle 2001 and Paloma 2008 both moved northeast in the Caribbean. But perhaps the most interesting example of an easterly moving hurricane in the tropics is Lenny in 1999, which formed near Jamaica, moved almost due east through the Caribbean, and hit the US and British Virgin Islands from the west as a Cat 4 hurricane.
